o Training for CT technologists
o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol
o Human resource availability
o Communication processes with EVT Centre experts
Abbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue
plasminogen activator.
process text: 
<process>
  <activity role='triage nurse or receiving clinician' action='identify suspected stroke presentation and activate early assessment pathway' objects='patient with suspected stroke' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
    <branch id='branch19'>
      <activity role='acute stroke team' action='assess airway' objects='airway patency and immediate airway support needs' id='activity2_1'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch20'>
      <activity role='acute stroke team' action='assess breathing' objects='respiratory status and immediate breathing support needs' id='activity2_2'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch21'>
      <activity role='acute stroke team' action='assess circulation' objects='hemodynamic status and immediate circulatory support needs' id='activity2_3'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='clinician' action='perform neurological examination and assess stroke severity on a standardized stroke scale' objects='focal neurological deficits, NIHSS or CNS for stroke' id='activity3'/>
  <activity role='clinical team' action='make patient NPO initially' objects='oral medication, fluid, food restriction' id='activity4'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway3'>
        <branch id='branch22'>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform rapid non-contrast brain imaging within the acute stroke treatment time window' objects='non-contrast CT or MRI' id='activity5_1'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch23'>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform CTA within the acute stroke treatment time window' objects='CTA, door to CT/CTA less than 25 minutes' id='activity5_2'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <activity role='stroke physician or radiology expert' action='interpret brain imaging immediately' objects='CT, CTA, and or MRI of the brain' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='nurse or technician' action='perform ECG without delaying acute treatment' objects='ECG for atrial fibrillation and other acute arrhythmias' id='activity7'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='laboratory team' action='obtain urgent blood work' objects='CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, glucose, INR, partial thromboplastin time, troponin test if clinically indicated' id='activity8'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4'>
      <activity role='nurse or trained clinician' action='screen swallowing ability as part of the initial assessment and before initiating oral intake' objects='simple, valid, reliable bedside swallowing test protocol' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='avoid delaying thrombolysis assessment' objects='chest x-ray' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
    <branch condition='stroke type is intracerebral hemorrhage' id='branch10'>
      <activity role='stroke physician' action='treat as a medical emergency and evaluate immediately' objects='ICH patient, stroke management expertise' id='activity16'/>
      <activity role='physician' action='ask about anticoagulant therapy and evaluate coagulation status' objects='anticoagulant therapy history, platelet count, PTT, INR' id='activity17'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='selected patient with ICH requires further vascular assessment' id='branch11'>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform vascular imaging as soon as possible' objects='CTA or MRA' id='activity18'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='selected patient with ICH does not require further vascular assessment' id='branch12'>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='continue ICH management without CTA or MRA' objects='ICH care plan' id='activity19'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='stroke type is not intracerebral hemorrhage' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='continue ischemic stroke or TIA assessment pathway' objects='non-ICH patient' id='activity20'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='patient presents with potentially disabling acute neurological symptoms suggestive of acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset or may be treated within a 12-hour window and meets clinical and imaging criteria for EVT' id='branch6'>
          <activity role='stroke physician' action='confirm EVT eligibility based on clinical criteria' objects='potentially disabling symptoms, symptom onset timing, treatment window, clinical eligibility' id='activity11_1'/>
          <activity role='stroke physician' action='confirm EVT eligibility based on imaging criteria' objects='brain imaging and vascular imaging findings for EVT selection' id='activity11_2'/>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='initiate urgent communication for treatment and transport decision-making' objects='EVT pathway, receiving EVT centre' id='activity12'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient does not meet EVT time window or clinical and imaging criteria' id='branch7'>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='continue non-EVT acute stroke pathway' objects='standard acute stroke management' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch condition='acute ischemic stroke is clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='consider advanced CT imaging' objects='CT and multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT' id='activity14'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='acute ischemic stroke is not clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments' id='branch9'>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='proceed without advanced acute treatment imaging' objects='standard imaging results' id='activity15'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
    <branch condition='patient presents beyond 48 hours with TIA or ischemic stroke' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='imaging team' action='perform vascular imaging as soon as possible' objects='brain and neck arteries' id='activity21'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient does not present beyond 48 hours with TIA or ischemic stroke' id='branch15'>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='continue pathway without this beyond-48-hours vascular imaging step' objects='current assessment findings' id='activity22'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='patient is not alert within the first 24 hours' id='branch16'>
      <activity role='nursing team' action='monitor closely and screen swallowing ability when clinically appropriate' objects='patient status during the first 24 hours, swallowing ability' id='activity23'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient presents with features indicating dysphagia' id='branch17'>
      <activity role='speech-language pathologist or appropriately trained specialist' action='perform full clinical swallowing assessment and advise on swallowing ability' objects='swallowing function, required consistency of diet and fluids' id='activity24'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient presents with features indicating pulmonary aspiration' id='branch18'>
      <activity role='speech-language pathologist or appropriately trained specialist' action='perform full clinical swallowing assessment and advise on swallowing ability' objects='pulmonary aspiration features, required consistency of diet and fluids' id='activity25'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='clinical team' action='provide secondary prevention assessment' objects='cross-continuum secondary prevention needs, admitted or discharged patient' id='activity26_1'/>
  <activity role='clinical team' action='initiate secondary prevention therapies' objects='secondary prevention assessments and therapies, Modules 5 and 10' id='activity26_2'/>
</process>
suggestion:
Problem: The parallel gateway with id='parallelgateway3' is an unreasonable design because 'perform rapid non-contrast brain imaging' (id='activity5_1') and 'perform CTA' (id='activity5_2') are modeled as parallel activities, while the recommendation says patients should undergo rapid brain imaging and CTA, not necessarily as two independently parallel tasks. In practice, these imaging steps are coordinated components of the imaging workflow and may be performed sequentially or as one combined imaging workflow depending on protocol and modality. Modeling them as a parallel gateway can create logic ambiguity and execution inconsistency.

Suggested modification: Replace the parallel gateway with id='parallelgateway3' by sequential activities under branch id='branch1'. For example, keep:
- <activity role='imaging team' action='perform rapid brain imaging within the acute stroke treatment time window' objects='non-contrast CT or MRI' id='activity5_1'/>
- <activity role='imaging team' action='perform CTA within the acute stroke treatment time window when indicated by the acute stroke imaging protocol' objects='CTA, door to CT/CTA less than 25 minutes' id='activity5_2'/>
followed by:
- <activity role='stroke physician or radiology expert' action='interpret brain imaging immediately' objects='CT, CTA, and or MRI of the brain' id='activity6'/>

Reason: The guideline requires urgent completion of both imaging components within target time, but it does not require them to be modeled as truly parallel branches. A sequential structure better reflects operational reality, avoids implying simultaneous scanner actions, and keeps the interpretation step after imaging acquisition is complete.
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

[2026-06-04 20:35:07 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Team leader on : ProcessGeneration, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 1: Early Assessment
This module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically
present at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients
are directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform
clinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway.
Module 1 Recommended Practices 
1.1 Initial evaluation
1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway,
breathing, and circulation
1.2 Initial examinations
1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to
determine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a
standardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke)
1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological
symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom
onset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be
initiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who
meet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular
therapy
1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute
stroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging
(non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less
than twenty five minutes)
1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible
for acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and
multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered.
1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care
provider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of
the brain
1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and
other acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment
1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis 
1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work:
• CBC
• electrolytes
• creatinine
• glucose
• INR
• partial thromboplastin time
• troponin test (if clinically indicated)
1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia
1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have
their swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside
testing protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating
oral medication, fluid, or food
1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours
should be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be
screened when clinically appropriate
1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia
or pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of
their swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or
appropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability
and the required consistency of diet and fluids
1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the
ED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients
1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke
1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients
1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or
ischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and
neck arteries as soon as possible
1.7 Initial examinations for ICH
1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH
should be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated
immediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management
1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions
about anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and
INR
1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as
possible
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale;
CT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT,
Endovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI,
magnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke
Scale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient
ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as
possible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute
neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a
potential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT)
• Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following:
o The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute
stroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT.
o Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements
o Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall
Ontario.
o Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process.
• Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a
greater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre
• Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district
stroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke
• The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better
established to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information
• Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks
across the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care
• Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical
redirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and
management.
• Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the
ED (for appropriate patients)
• Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital
EDs
• Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain
imaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time
of less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for
appropriate patients
• Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can be diagnosed and treated in a timely
manner
• To facilitate early assessment, hospital-level CTAS I or CTAS II access to diagnostic imaging should be established
for suspected stroke patients
• Rapid turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic
stroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment
• Efforts to raise public awareness about the symptoms of stroke and when to contact 911 should continue to be
enhanced and funded
• Capacity planning to ensure access to CT/CTA should include:
o Training for CT technologists
o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol
o Human resource availability
o Communication processes with EVT Centre experts
Abbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue
plasminogen activator..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

          <activity role='acute care team' action='proceed according to bedside swallowing screen result' objects='oral intake plan' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch13'>
      <activity role='acute care clinician' action='ensure chest x-ray does not delay assessment for thrombolysis' objects='chest x-ray if ordered' id='activity17'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
    <branch condition='potentially disabling acute neurological symptoms suggestive of acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset or treatment can be initiated within a 12-hour window and clinical and imaging criteria are met' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='stroke physician' action='consider patient for endovascular therapy' objects='clinical criteria imaging criteria and symptom onset window up to 6.0 hours or treatable within 12 hours' id='activity18'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='does not meet endovascular therapy consideration criteria' id='branch15'>
      <activity role='stroke physician' action='continue with non-EVT acute stroke management decision-making' objects='clinical assessment and imaging findings' id='activity19'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>

          <activity role='stroke physician' action='continue ICH management without additional CTA or MRA' objects='ICH assessment findings' id='activity23'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>

      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>

          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform vascular imaging as soon as possible' objects='brain and neck arteries' id='activity24'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='not TIA or ischemic stroke beyond 48 hours' id='branch21'>
          <activity role='acute stroke team' action='continue pathway according to stroke subtype and timing' objects='assessment and imaging results' id='activity25'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>

      <activity role='stroke care team' action='provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies' objects='admitted patient secondary prevention plan Modules 5 and 10' id='activity26'/>

    <branch condition='patient is discharged from the ED' id='branch23'>
      <activity role='ED team' action='provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies' objects='discharged patient secondary prevention plan Modules 5 and 10' id='activity27'/>

| **need_reflect** | False |
| **assistant_role_name** | Process design expert |
| **user_role_name** | Team leader |
| **phase_prompt** | task: {task} process text: {refinement_process_text} Check which activities in the process text can be expanded into sub-activities and add these sub-activities to the process text. Please note: 1) Only expand important activities, not all of them. 2) Ensure that the process text remains logical when adding sub-activities. 3) Add appropriate gateways when adding activities. 4) When modifying the process text, note whether the newly added activity is a sequential, exclusive, or concurrent relationship. 5) Only expand activities if it's necessary and do not create overly detailed sub-activities. Here are some examples of expanding activities in process texts into sub-activities. You can refer to them but cannot copy them: 1. 'payment with card/cash' activity with id='activity2' can be expanded to:    sub-activity:'Payment with card' and 'Payment with cash'     activity relations: exclusive 2. 'club interview id and confimation email' activity with id='activity5' can be expanded to:    sub-activity:'Interview at the club' and 'Send confirmation email'    activity relations: sequential 3. 'Submit the materials' activity with id='activity9' can be expanded to:    sub-activity:'Submit interview materials' and 'Submit examination materials'     activity relations: parallel After extending the activity, number the activity or gateway in addition to the original id. If the sub-activity are exclusive or parallel relationships, you need to add them in the form of gateways when modifying the process text: {format} First output which activities can be extended according to the above format (...... can be expanded to:.......) and then give the modified process text. Don't say anything else. |

| **placeholders** | {'task': 'Module 1: Early Assessment\nThis module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically\npresent at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients\nare directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform\nclinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway.\nModule 1 Recommended Practices \n1.1 Initial evaluation\n1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway,\nbreathing, and circulation\n1.2 Initial examinations\n1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to\ndetermine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a\nstandardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke)\n1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological\nsymptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom\nonset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be\ninitiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who\nmeet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular\ntherapy\n1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute\nstroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging\n(non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less\nthan twenty five minutes)\n1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible\nfor acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and\nmultiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered.\n1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care\nprovider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of\nthe brain\n1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and\nother acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment\n1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis \n1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work:\n• CBC\n• electrolytes\n• creatinine\n• glucose\n• INR\n• partial thromboplastin time\n• troponin test (if clinically indicated)\n1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia\n1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have\ntheir swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside\ntesting protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating\noral medication, fluid, or food\n1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours\nshould be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be\nscreened a speech-language pathologist or\nappropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability\nand the required consistency of diet and fluids\n1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment ICH\n1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI,\nmagnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke\nScale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient\nischemic attack.\n\nThe following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.\n\nModule 1 Implementation Considerations\nGeneral considerations\n• Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as\npossible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute\nneurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a\npotential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy acute\nstroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT.\no Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements\no Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall\nOntario.\no Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process.\n• Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a\ngreater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre\n• Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district\nstroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke\n• The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better\nestablished to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information\n• Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks\nacross the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care\n• Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical\nredirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and\nmanagement.\n• Upon brain\nimaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time\nof less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to | **task_prompt** | Module 1: Early Assessment This module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically present at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients are directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform clinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway. Module 1 Recommended Practices  1.1 Initial evaluation 1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway, breathing, and circulation 1.2 Initial examinations 1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to determine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a standardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke) 1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be initiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who meet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular therapy 1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute stroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging (non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less than twenty five minutes) 1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered. 1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care provider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of the brain 1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and other acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment 1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis  1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work: • CBC • electrolytes • creatinine • glucose • INR • partial thromboplastin time • troponin test (if clinically indicated) 1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia 1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have their swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside testing protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating oral medication, fluid, or food 1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours should be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be screened when clinically appropriate 1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia or pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of their swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or appropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability and the required consistency of diet and fluids 1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the ED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients 1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke 1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients 1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or ischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and neck arteries as soon as possible 1.7 Initial examinations for ICH 1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH should be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated immediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management 1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions about anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and INR 1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as possible Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale; CT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT, Endovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI, magnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke Scale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 1 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as possible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a potential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT) • Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following: o The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute stroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT. o Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements o Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall Ontario. o Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process. • Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a greater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre • Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district stroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke • The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better established to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information • Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks across the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care • Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical redirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and management. • Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the ED (for appropriate patients) • Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital EDs • Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain imaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time of less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for appropriate patients • Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can be diagnosed and treated in a timely manner • To facilitate early assessment, hospital-level CTAS I or CTAS II access to diagnostic imaging should be established for suspected stroke patients • Rapid turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic stroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment • Efforts to raise public awareness about the symptoms of stroke and when to contact 911 should continue to be enhanced and funded • Capacity planning to ensure access to CT/CTA should include: o Training for CT technologists o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol o Human resource availability o Communication processes with EVT Centre experts Abbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue plasminogen activator. |

[2026-06-04 20:35:07 INFO] flask app.py did not start for online log
[2026-06-04 20:35:07 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 1: Early Assessment
This module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically
present at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients
are directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform
clinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway.
Module 1 Recommended Practices 
1.1 Initial evaluation
1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway,
breathing, and circulation
1.2 Initial examinations
1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to
determine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a
standardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke)
1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological
symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom
onset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be
initiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who
meet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular
therapy
1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute
stroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging
(non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less
than twenty five minutes)
1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible
for acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and
multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered.
1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care
provider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of
the brain
1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and
other acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment
1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis 
1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work:
• CBC
• electrolytes
• creatinine
• glucose
• INR
• partial thromboplastin time
• troponin test (if clinically indicated)
1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia
1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have
their swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside
testing protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating
oral medication, fluid, or food
1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours
should be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be
screened when clinically appropriate
1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia
or pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of
their swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or
appropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability
and the required consistency of diet and fluids
1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the
ED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients
1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke
1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients
1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or
ischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and
neck arteries as soon as possible
1.7 Initial examinations for ICH
1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH
should be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated
immediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management
1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions
about anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and
INR
1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as
possible
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale;
CT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT,
Endovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI,
magnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke
Scale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient
ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as
possible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute
neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a
potential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT)
• Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following:
o The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute
stroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT.
o Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements
o Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall
Ontario.
o Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process.
• Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a
greater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre
• Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district
stroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke
• The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better
established to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information
• Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks
across the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care
• Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical
redirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and
management.
• Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the
ED (for appropriate patients)
• Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital
EDs
• Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain
imaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time
of less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for
appropriate patients
• Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can be diagnosed and treated in a timely
manner
• To facilitate early assessment, hospital-level CTAS I or CTAS II access to diagnostic imaging should be established
for suspected stroke patients
• Rapid turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic
stroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment
• Efforts to raise public awareness about the symptoms of stroke and when to contact 911 should continue to be
enhanced and funded
• Capacity planning to ensure access to CT/CTA should include:
o Training for CT technologists
o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol
o Human resource availability
o Communication processes with EVT Centre experts
Abbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue
plasminogen activator..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 1: Early Assessment
This module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically
present at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients
are directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform
clinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway.
Module 1 Recommended Practices 
1.1 Initial evaluation
1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway,
breathing, and circulation
1.2 Initial examinations
1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to
determine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a
standardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke)
1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological
symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom
onset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be
initiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who
meet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular
therapy
1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute
stroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging
(non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less
than twenty five minutes)
1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible
for acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and
multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered.
1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care
provider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of
the brain
1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and
other acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment
1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis 
1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work:
• CBC
• electrolytes
• creatinine
• glucose
• INR
• partial thromboplastin time
• troponin test (if clinically indicated)
1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia
1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have
their swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside
testing protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating
oral medication, fluid, or food
1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours
should be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be
screened when clinically appropriate
1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia
or pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of
their swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or
appropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability
and the required consistency of diet and fluids
1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the
ED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients
1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke
1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients
1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or
ischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and
neck arteries as soon as possible
1.7 Initial examinations for ICH
1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH
should be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated
immediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management
1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions
about anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and
INR
1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as
possible
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale;
CT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT,
Endovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI,
magnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke
Scale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient
ischemic attack.

o Training for CT technologists
o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol
o Human resource availability

plasminogen activator.
process text:
<process>
  <activity role='triage clinician' action='identify suspected stroke presentation and initiate early assessment pathway' objects='suspected stroke patient at ED outpatient clinic or direct acute care admission' id='activity1'/>
  <activity role='acute care clinician' action='conduct rapid initial evaluation' objects='airway breathing and circulation' id='activity2'/>
  <activity role='acute stroke team' action='perform neurological examination and assess stroke severity on standardized stroke scale' objects='focal neurological deficits NIHSS or CNS' id='activity3'/>
  <activity role='acute care clinician' action='make patient NPO initially' objects='oral medication fluid and food restriction' id='activity4'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='presenting within acute stroke treatment time window' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform rapid brain imaging and vascular imaging with door to CT or CTA less than 25 minutes' objects='non-contrast CT or MRI and CTA' id='activity5'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
            <branch condition='acute ischemic stroke clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments' id='branch3'>
              <activity role='imaging team' action='consider advanced CT imaging' objects='CT and multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT' id='activity6'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='not clinically eligible for acute ischemic stroke acute treatment advanced imaging' id='branch4'>
              <activity role='acute stroke team' action='continue standard imaging pathway' objects='initial brain imaging results' id='activity7'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='presenting beyond acute stroke treatment time window' id='branch5'>
          <activity role='acute stroke team' action='continue non-hyperacute assessment pathway' objects='clinical history and examination findings' id='activity8'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='qualified imaging interpreter' action='interpret brain imaging immediately' objects='CT CTA and or MRI of the brain' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch6'>
      <activity role='cardiac assessment team' action='perform ECG without delaying acute treatment' objects='electrocardiogram to detect atrial fibrillation and other acute arrhythmias' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch7'>
      <activity role='laboratory team' action='obtain and process blood work urgently' objects='CBC electrolytes creatinine glucose INR PTT and troponin test if clinically indicated' id='activity11'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch8'>
      <activity role='acute care clinician' action='screen swallowing ability before initiating oral medication fluid or food using a simple valid reliable bedside testing protocol' objects='swallowing function' id='activity12'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='patient not alert within the first 24 hours' id='branch9'>
          <activity role='acute care team' action='monitor closely and screen swallowing ability when clinically appropriate' objects='level of alertness and swallowing ability within first 24 hours' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient alert within the first 24 hours' id='branch10'>
          <activity role='acute care team' action='continue routine swallowing precautions after initial screen' objects='swallowing status' id='activity14'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='features indicating dysphagia or pulmonary aspiration are present' id='branch11'>
          <activity role='speech-language pathologist or appropriately trained specialist' action='perform full clinical swallowing assessment and advise on swallowing ability and required consistency' objects='diet and fluids consistency' id='activity15'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='no features indicating dysphagia or pulmonary aspiration' id='branch12'>
          <activity role='acute care team' action='proceed according to bedside swallowing screen result' objects='oral intake plan' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch13'>
      <activity role='acute care clinician' action='ensure chest x-ray does not delay assessment for thrombolysis' objects='chest x-ray if ordered' id='activity17'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
    <branch condition='potentially disabling acute neurological symptoms suggestive of acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset or treatment can be initiated within a 12-hour window and clinical and imaging criteria are met' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='stroke physician' action='consider patient for endovascular therapy' objects='clinical criteria imaging criteria and symptom onset window up to 6.0 hours or treatable within 12 hours' id='activity18'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='does not meet endovascular therapy consideration criteria' id='branch15'>
      <activity role='stroke physician' action='continue with non-EVT acute stroke management decision-making' objects='clinical assessment and imaging findings' id='activity19'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
    <branch condition='diagnosis is intracerebral hemorrhage' id='branch16'>
      <activity role='stroke physician with expertise in stroke management' action='treat ICH as a medical emergency and evaluate immediately' objects='patient with ICH' id='activity20'/>
      <activity role='stroke physician' action='ask about anticoagulant therapy and evaluate coagulation-related investigations' objects='anticoagulant therapy history platelet count PTT and INR' id='activity21'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
        <branch condition='selected patient with ICH' id='branch17'>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform vascular imaging as soon as possible' objects='CTA or MRA' id='activity22'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='not a selected patient for additional vascular imaging' id='branch18'>
          <activity role='stroke physician' action='continue ICH management without additional CTA or MRA' objects='ICH assessment findings' id='activity23'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='diagnosis is TIA or ischemic stroke or stroke type other than ICH' id='branch19'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>
        <branch condition='TIA or ischemic stroke presenting beyond 48 hours' id='branch20'>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform vascular imaging as soon as possible' objects='brain and neck arteries' id='activity24'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='not TIA or ischemic stroke beyond 48 hours' id='branch21'>
          <activity role='acute stroke team' action='continue pathway according to stroke subtype and timing' objects='assessment and imaging results' id='activity25'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='patient is admitted to hospital' id='branch22'>
      <activity role='stroke care team' action='provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies' objects='admitted patient secondary prevention plan Modules 5 and 10' id='activity26'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient is discharged from the ED' id='branch23'>
      <activity role='ED team' action='provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies' objects='discharged patient secondary prevention plan Modules 5 and 10' id='activity27'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
</process>
Check which activities in the process text can be expanded into sub-activities and add these sub-activities to the process text. Please note:
1) Only expand important activities, not all of them.
2) Ensure that the process text remains logical when adding sub-activities.
3) Add appropriate gateways when adding activities.
4) When modifying the process text, note whether the newly added activity is a sequential, exclusive, or concurrent relationship.
5) Only expand activities if it's necessary and do not create overly detailed sub-activities.
Here are some examples of expanding activities in process texts into sub-activities. You can refer to them but cannot copy them:
1. 'payment with card/cash' activity with id='activity2' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Payment with card' and 'Payment with cash' 
   activity relations: exclusive
2. 'club interview id and confimation email' activity with id='activity5' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Interview at the club' and 'Send confirmation email'
   activity relations: sequential
3. 'Submit the materials' activity with id='activity9' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Submit interview materials' and 'Submit examination materials' 
   activity relations: parallel
After extending the activity, number the activity or gateway in addition to the original id. If the sub-activity are exclusive or parallel relationships, you need to add them in the form of gateways when modifying the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

First output which activities can be extended according to the above format (...... can be expanded to:.......) and then give the modified process text. Don't say anything else.
